query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the **Kea DHCP server** replaces the ISC DHCP server as the supported solution.

Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers. It offers advantages such as an extensible server solution with module hooks and re-configuration capabilities through a REST API.

Sources:
- [10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. Module and Plugin Improvements Ansible Core 2.20 includes a number of practical improvements to built-in modules and plugins: blockinfile and lineinfile module — A new encoding option has been added, enabling support for files in encodings other than UTF-8. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. The top-level lvs fact deduplicates logical volume names across volume groups and may be incomplete, making this new per-VG view essential for environments with complex LVM configurations. default callback plugin — A new option allows you to configure indentation for JSON and YAML output, giving you control over how task output is formatted for readability. deb822_repository module — Automatic installation of the python3-debian package when missing, reducing prerequisites for managing APT repositories on Debian-based target nodes. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. Customers should explicitly set their transport to ssh if not already doing so. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

RHEL System Roles for SAP development is based on the Content from sap-linuxlab.github.io is not included. SAP LinuxLab upstream project. In contrast, RHEL System Roles for SAP is provided in the RHEL for SAP Solutions subscription. The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. However, if you require full support for Ansible Core, a separate subscription is necessary. Additional information is available at Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) System Roles . The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are fully supported on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_general_preconfigure sap_netweaver_preconfigure sap_hana_preconfigure sap_hana_install The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are supported as Technology Preview on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_ha_install_hana_hsr sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster sap_swpm (*) The RHEL System Roles for SAP, just like the RHEL System Roles , are installed and run from a central node referred to as the control node (which can be Content from www.ansible.com is not included. Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Note Package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 does not contain the role sap_swpm . This role is planned to be released in the next version of package rhel-system-roles-sap . Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Overview Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 7 RHEA-2019:3190 introduced RHEL System Roles for SAP to assist with remotely or locally configuring a RHEL system for the installation of SAP HANA or SAP NetWeaver software. RHEL System Roles for SAP development is based on the Content from sap-linuxlab.github.io is not included. SAP LinuxLab upstream project. RHEL System Roles is a collection of roles executed by Ansible to assist administrators with RHEL system configuration. These roles are provided in the RHEL AppStream repository. In contrast, RHEL System Roles for SAP is provided in the RHEL for SAP Solutions subscription. The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. However, if you require full support for Ansible Core, a separate subscription is necessary. Additional information is available at Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) System Roles . The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are fully supported on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_general_preconfigure sap_netweaver_preconfigure sap_hana_preconfigure sap_hana_install The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are supported as Technology Preview on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_ha_install_hana_hsr sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster sap_swpm (*) The RHEL System Roles for SAP, just like the RHEL System Roles , are installed and run from a central node referred to as the control node (which can be Content from www.ansible.com is not included. Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Note Package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 does not contain the role sap_swpm . This role is planned to be released in the next version of package rhel-system-roles-sap . Purpose Role Name Description sap_general_preconfigure Perform installation and configuration steps common to SAP NetWeaver and SAP HANA sap_netweaver_preconfigure Perform additional installation and configuration steps for SAP NetWeaver sap_hana_preconfigure Perform additional installation and configuration for SAP HANA sap_hana_install Perform a SAP HANA scale-up or scale-out installation sap_ha_install_hana_hsr Set up SAP HANA System Replication on two nodes sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster Install Pacemaker and configure the cluster and SAP cluster resources sap_swpm Install SAP Software via SWPM Support Status Role Name Support Status Remote Host Management Control Node  [...]

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Note Package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 does not contain the role sap_swpm . This role is planned to be released in the next version of package rhel-system-roles-sap . Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Making open source more inclusive Red Hat is committed to replacing problematic language in our code and documentation. ... Include links to the relevant parts of the documentation. If you want to be notified about future updates, please make sure you are assigned as Reporter . Click Create at the bottom of the dialogue. Chapter 1. Overview Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 7 RHEA-2019:3190 introduced RHEL System Roles for SAP to assist with remotely or locally configuring a RHEL system for the installation of SAP HANA or SAP NetWeaver software. RHEL System Roles for SAP development is based on the Content from sap-linuxlab.github.io is not included. SAP LinuxLab upstream project. RHEL System Roles is a collection of roles executed by Ansible to assist administrators with RHEL system configuration. These roles are provided in the RHEL AppStream repository. In contrast, RHEL System Roles for SAP is provided in the RHEL for SAP Solutions subscription. The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. However, if you require full support for Ansible Core, a separate subscription is necessary. Additional information is available at Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) System Roles . The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are fully supported on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_general_preconfigure sap_netweaver_preconfigure sap_hana_preconfigure sap_hana_install The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are supported as Technology Preview on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_ha_install_hana_hsr sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster sap_swpm (*) The RHEL System Roles for SAP, just like the RHEL System Roles , are installed and run from a central node referred to as the control node (which can be Content from www.ansible.com is not included. Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Note Package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 does not contain the role sap_swpm . This role is planned to be released in the next version of package rhel-system-roles-sap . Purpose Role Name Description sap_general_preconfigure Perform installation and configuration steps common to SAP NetWeaver and SAP HANA sap_netweaver_preconfigure Perform additional installation and configuration steps for SAP NetWeaver sap_hana_preconfigure Perform additional installation and configuration for SAP HANA sap_hana_install Perform a SAP HANA scale-up or scale-out installation sap_ha_install_hana_hsr Set up SAP HANA System Replication on two nodes sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster Install Pacemaker and configure the cluster and SAP cluster resources sap_swpm Install SAP Software via SWPM Support Status Role Name Support Status Remote Host Management Control Node  [...]

Type: Solution

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. **Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6736391
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Python 3 Issue Which Python version is supported in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Which is the default Python version in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Resolution Python 3.9 will be the default Python version in RHEL 9. It will be preinstalled and will be supported for the entire lifecycle for RHEL 9.To reduce the complexity with python installations, it will be installed as set of Non-modular RPMs. Note : The /usr/libexec/platform-python is implemented as a symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3 . Although this platform-python symlink is deprecated in RHEL 9 and is added for backwards compatibility with RHEL 8. The /usr/bin/python3 is used by Red Hat shipped applications and is recommended to be used for third party applications, User scripts and custom use of python library instead of /usr/libexec/platform-python The /usr/bin/python3 and /usr/bin/python3.9 are present by default. The /usr/bin/python , if present or installed later, will have a symlink to /usr/bin/python3 (uninstallable). The symlinks from /usr/bin/python and /usr/bin/python3 ensure the same version Python 3.9.9 is used. $ ls -l /usr/libexec/platform-python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 18 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/libexec/platform-python -> /usr/bin/python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:18 /usr/bin/python -> ./python3 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3 lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3.9 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 16072 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3.9 Python 3.9+ versions as become available will be allowed for parallel installations to co-exist with default python 3.9 . These will be Non-Modular. Python and pip without specifying the version will point to default 3.9 Python and pip , while it will also allow using python3 and pip3 . Python 2 is not planned to be shipped with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/715683

Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6736391
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Python 3 Issue Which Python version is supported in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Which is the default Python version in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Resolution Python 3.9 will be the default Python version in RHEL 9. It will be preinstalled and will be supported for the entire lifecycle for RHEL 9.To reduce the complexity with python installations, it will be installed as set of Non-modular RPMs. Note : The /usr/libexec/platform-python is implemented as a symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3 . Although this platform-python symlink is deprecated in RHEL 9 and is added for backwards compatibility with RHEL 8. The /usr/bin/python3 is used by Red Hat shipped applications and is recommended to be used for third party applications, User scripts and custom use of python library instead of /usr/libexec/platform-python The /usr/bin/python3 and /usr/bin/python3.9 are present by default. The /usr/bin/python , if present or installed later, will have a symlink to /usr/bin/python3 (uninstallable). The symlinks from /usr/bin/python and /usr/bin/python3 ensure the same version Python 3.9.9 is used. $ ls -l /usr/libexec/platform-python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 18 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/libexec/platform-python -> /usr/bin/python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:18 /usr/bin/python -> ./python3 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3 lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3.9 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 16072 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3.9 Python 3.9+ versions as become available will be allowed for parallel installations to co-exist with default python 3.9 . These will be Non-Modular. Python and pip without specifying the version will point to default 3.9 Python and pip , while it will also allow using python3 and pip3 . Python 2 is not planned to be shipped with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6984287
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.
