**How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/715683
Last updated: 2024-08-05
Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6736391
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Python 3 Issue Which Python version is supported in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Which is the default Python version in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Resolution Python 3.9 will be the default Python version in RHEL 9. It will be preinstalled and will be supported for the entire lifecycle for RHEL 9.To reduce the complexity with python installations, it will be installed as set of Non-modular RPMs. Note : The /usr/libexec/platform-python is implemented as a symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3 . Although this platform-python symlink is deprecated in RHEL 9 and is added for backwards compatibility with RHEL 8. The /usr/bin/python3 is used by Red Hat shipped applications and is recommended to be used for third party applications, User scripts and custom use of python library instead of /usr/libexec/platform-python The /usr/bin/python3 and /usr/bin/python3.9 are present by default. The /usr/bin/python , if present or installed later, will have a symlink to /usr/bin/python3 (uninstallable). The symlinks from /usr/bin/python and /usr/bin/python3 ensure the same version Python 3.9.9 is used. $ ls -l /usr/libexec/platform-python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 18 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/libexec/platform-python -> /usr/bin/python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:18 /usr/bin/python -> ./python3 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3 lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3.9 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 16072 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3.9 Python 3.9+ versions as become available will be allowed for parallel installations to co-exist with default python 3.9 . These will be Non-Modular. Python and pip without specifying the version will point to default 3.9 Python and pip , while it will also allow using python3 and pip3 . Python 2 is not planned to be shipped with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6984287
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

With the introduction of Red Hat Enterprise Linux version 8, Red Hat simplified the RHEL product phases from four to three: Full Support, Maintenance Support, and Extended Life Phase. We are also providing information on anticipated release dates and minor releases that will offer extended support. Red Hat Enterprise Linux Version 8, 9, and 10 deliver a ten year life cycle in Full Support and Maintenance Support Phases followed by an Extended Life Phase. Life Cycle iv : The Red Hat Enterprise Linux life cycle phases are designed to reduce the level of change within each major release i over time and make release availability and content more predictable. ii Details During the life cycle for each major version, software changes to Red Hat Enterprise Linux are delivered via individual updates known as errata advisories through the Red Hat Customer Portal or other authorized Red Hat portals. ... Errata advisories are tested and qualified against the active Red Hat Enterprise Linux major release. (For example, a Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 RHSA will be applied cumulatively to the latest Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 version and patch set.) Released errata advisories remain accessible to active subscribers for the entire Red Hat Enterprise Linux life cycle. Within each major release of Red Hat Enterprise Linux, any errata advisory (including one released as part of a minor release) will be applied cumulatively to the latest release of Red Hat Enterprise Linux, including any patch sets. During the life cycle of a major Red Hat Enterprise Linux release, Red Hat makes commercially reasonable efforts to maintain binary compatibility for the core runtime environment across all minor releases and errata advisories. ... Red Hat may choose, as a temporary measure, to address those catastrophic issues with a significant customer business impact with a Hotfix while the Bug Fix errata advisory (RHBA) is being created. Native hardware enablement is provided by backporting hardware drivers, etc., to the relevant version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux. Hardware enablement using virtualization is achieved by running an earlier version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux as a virtual guest on a newer version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux. See the Virtualization description below for details. NOTE: Hardware certification (including the associated hardware limits) applies to the version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux that is being used as the host. ... The focus for minor releases during this phase lies on resolving defects of medium or higher priority. Updated installation images will be provided for minor releases during the Full Support Phase. **Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6736391
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Python 3 Issue Which Python version is supported in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Which is the default Python version in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Resolution Python 3.9 will be the default Python version in RHEL 9. It will be preinstalled and will be supported for the entire lifecycle for RHEL 9.To reduce the complexity with python installations, it will be installed as set of Non-modular RPMs. Note : The /usr/libexec/platform-python is implemented as a symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3 . Although this platform-python symlink is deprecated in RHEL 9 and is added for backwards compatibility with RHEL 8. The /usr/bin/python3 is used by Red Hat shipped applications and is recommended to be used for third party applications, User scripts and custom use of python library instead of /usr/libexec/platform-python The /usr/bin/python3 and /usr/bin/python3.9 are present by default. The /usr/bin/python , if present or installed later, will have a symlink to /usr/bin/python3 (uninstallable). The symlinks from /usr/bin/python and /usr/bin/python3 ensure the same version Python 3.9.9 is used. $ ls -l /usr/libexec/platform-python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 18 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/libexec/platform-python -> /usr/bin/python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:18 /usr/bin/python -> ./python3 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3 lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3.9 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 16072 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3.9 Python 3.9+ versions as become available will be allowed for parallel installations to co-exist with default python 3.9 . These will be Non-Modular. Python and pip without specifying the version will point to default 3.9 Python and pip , while it will also allow using python3 and pip3 . Python 2 is not planned to be shipped with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6984287
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated?**

Applicability: RHEL

Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle Overview Details Production Phases Full Support Phase Maintenance Support Phase Extended Life Phase Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9 Life Cycle RHEL 8 Planning Guide RHEL 9 Planning Guide RHEL 10 Planning Guide Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle Red Hat Enterprise Linux Extended Maintenance Extended Update Support Add-on Enhanced Extended Update Support Add-on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 EUS Maintenance Policy; and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 EUS/Enhanced EUS Maintenance Policy Update Services for SAP Solutions Extended Life-cycle Support Add-On Virtualization Life-cycle Dates Retired Life Cycle Dates Other Related Products Overview Red Hat Enterprise Linux Version 8, 9, and 10 Red Hat understands the importance of life cycle planning for our customers, partners, ISVs, and the Red Hat Enterprise Linux ecosystem. With the introduction of Red Hat Enterprise Linux version 8, Red Hat simplified the RHEL product phases from four to three: Full Support, Maintenance Support, and Extended Life Phase. We are also providing information on anticipated release dates and minor releases that will offer extended support. Red Hat Enterprise Linux Version 8, 9, and 10 deliver a ten year life cycle in Full Support and Maintenance Support Phases followed by an Extended Life Phase. Life Cycle iv : The Red Hat Enterprise Linux life cycle phases are designed to reduce the level of change within each major release i over time and make release availability and content more predictable. ii Details During the life cycle for each major version, software changes to Red Hat Enterprise Linux are delivered via individual updates known as errata advisories through the Red Hat Customer Portal or other authorized Red Hat portals. ... Errata advisories are tested and qualified against the active Red Hat Enterprise Linux major release. (For example, a Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 RHSA will be applied cumulatively to the latest Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 version and patch set.) Released errata advisories remain accessible to active subscribers for the entire Red Hat Enterprise Linux life cycle. Within each major release of Red Hat Enterprise Linux, any errata advisory (including one released as part of a minor release) will be applied cumulatively to the latest release of Red Hat Enterprise Linux, including any patch sets. During the life cycle of a major Red Hat Enterprise Linux release, Red Hat makes commercially reasonable efforts to maintain binary compatibility for the core runtime environment across all minor releases and errata advisories. ... Red Hat may choose, as a temporary measure, to address those catastrophic issues with a significant customer business impact with a Hotfix while the Bug Fix errata advisory (RHBA) is being created. Native hardware enablement is provided by backporting hardware drivers, etc., to the relevant version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux. Hardware enablement using virtualization is achieved by running an earlier version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux as a virtual guest on a newer version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux. See the Virtualization description below for details. NOTE: Hardware certification (including the associated hardware limits) applies to the version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux that is being used as the host. ... The focus for minor releases during this phase lies on resolving defects of medium or higher priority. Updated installation images will be provided for minor releases during the Full Support Phase. Maintenance Support Phase : During the Maintenance Support Phase for Red Hat Enterprise Linux Version 8, 9, and 10, Critical, Important, and Moderate CVEs with a CVSS score of 7 or higher as defined by Red Hat in a Red Hat Security Advisory (RHSA) v ; and Urgent and Selected High Priority Bug Fix as defined by Red Hat in a Red Hat Bug Advisory (RHBA) will be released as they become available. Other errata advisories may be delivered as appropriate. New functionality and new hardware enablement are not planned for availability in the Maintenance Support Phase. ... No bug fixes, security fixes, hardware enablement or root-cause analysis will be available during this phase, and support will be provided on existing installations only. Red Hat reserves the right to terminate the ongoing support in the Extended Life Phase for a particular version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux at any time. **Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6736391
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Python 3 Issue Which Python version is supported in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Which is the default Python version in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Resolution Python 3.9 will be the default Python version in RHEL 9. It will be preinstalled and will be supported for the entire lifecycle for RHEL 9.To reduce the complexity with python installations, it will be installed as set of Non-modular RPMs. Note : The /usr/libexec/platform-python is implemented as a symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3 . Although this platform-python symlink is deprecated in RHEL 9 and is added for backwards compatibility with RHEL 8. The /usr/bin/python3 is used by Red Hat shipped applications and is recommended to be used for third party applications, User scripts and custom use of python library instead of /usr/libexec/platform-python The /usr/bin/python3 and /usr/bin/python3.9 are present by default. The /usr/bin/python , if present or installed later, will have a symlink to /usr/bin/python3 (uninstallable). The symlinks from /usr/bin/python and /usr/bin/python3 ensure the same version Python 3.9.9 is used. $ ls -l /usr/libexec/platform-python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 18 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/libexec/platform-python -> /usr/bin/python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:18 /usr/bin/python -> ./python3 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3 lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3.9 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 16072 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3.9 Python 3.9+ versions as become available will be allowed for parallel installations to co-exist with default python 3.9 . These will be Non-Modular. Python and pip without specifying the version will point to default 3.9 Python and pip , while it will also allow using python3 and pip3 . Python 2 is not planned to be shipped with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: Supported Python version on Openshift container platform nodes Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment OpenShift container platform 3.11 RedHat Enterprise Linux 7.7 Issue Do OpenShift Container Platform nodes have a dependency on Python2? Python 2.7.5 has EOL on 1 Jan 2020. Do we have to upgrade the python version? **Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6984287
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/715683
Last updated: 2024-08-05
Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6736391
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Which Python version is default in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 Python 3 Issue Which Python version is supported in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Which is the default Python version in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.x ? Resolution Python 3.9 will be the default Python version in RHEL 9. It will be preinstalled and will be supported for the entire lifecycle for RHEL 9.To reduce the complexity with python installations, it will be installed as set of Non-modular RPMs. Note : The /usr/libexec/platform-python is implemented as a symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3 . Although this platform-python symlink is deprecated in RHEL 9 and is added for backwards compatibility with RHEL 8. The /usr/bin/python3 is used by Red Hat shipped applications and is recommended to be used for third party applications, User scripts and custom use of python library instead of /usr/libexec/platform-python The /usr/bin/python3 and /usr/bin/python3.9 are present by default. The /usr/bin/python , if present or installed later, will have a symlink to /usr/bin/python3 (uninstallable). The symlinks from /usr/bin/python and /usr/bin/python3 ensure the same version Python 3.9.9 is used. $ ls -l /usr/libexec/platform-python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 18 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/libexec/platform-python -> /usr/bin/python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:18 /usr/bin/python -> ./python3 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3 lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 9 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.9 $ ls -l /usr/bin/python3.9 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 16072 Nov 23 23:08 /usr/bin/python3.9 Python 3.9+ versions as become available will be allowed for parallel installations to co-exist with default python 3.9 . These will be Non-Modular. Python and pip without specifying the version will point to default 3.9 Python and pip , while it will also allow using python3 and pip3 . Python 2 is not planned to be shipped with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: Supported Python version on Openshift container platform nodes Solution Verified - Updated 14 Jun 2024 Environment OpenShift container platform 3.11 RedHat Enterprise Linux 7.7 Issue Do OpenShift Container Platform nodes have a dependency on Python2? Python 2.7.5 has EOL on 1 Jan 2020. Do we have to upgrade the python version? Resolution Python can be upgraded to the latest version as it is supported by the underlying Operating System , but some of the important OpenShift packages and BaseOS still requires python2 for basic functionality. # yum install python3 It is recommended to keep both the versions of python i.e. python2 and python3. As of RHEL 7.7, python3 has included in base RHEL 7 channels without the need for software collections but this does not replace the need for having python2 as the system default python for OS utilities on RHEL 7. Root Cause Python2 can not be replaced completely as the system uses python2 for OS utilities on RHEL 7. Content: Ansible Tower output shows failure with wrong python version Solution Unverified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment RHEL 7 Ansible Tower 3.8.1 Python 2.7.5 Python3 Issue Even though Python3 is expected, when a playbook is run from Ansible Tower, an error in the job output indicates python 2.x is being used. ansible-playbook 2.9.27 config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg configured module search path = [u'/var/lib/awx/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules'] ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible executable location = /usr/bin/ansible-playbook python version = 2.7.5 (default, Aug 13 2020, 02:51:10) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-39)].. *output omitted*.PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************localhost : ok=1 changed=0 unreachable=0 failed=1 skipped=0 rescued=0 ignored=0 Resolution Install the desired version of Ansible in the virtual environment to be used. This install should bring along with it, the updated and correct version of Python. A second, more comprehensive, approach would be to delete the defunct environment (making sure nothing is being used in it) and create a new virtual environment, installing the more recent version of Ansible. Root Cause For normal operation, Ansible requires a Python interpreter to be installed on hosts running Linux. Ansible attempts to locate a Python interpreter on each host the first time a module is run on that host. In the problematic situation described in this solution, the venv (virtual environment) was created but Ansible was not installed within it, so the venv was defaulting to the RHEL server's (the one running Ansible Tower) default installation of Ansible (and therefore the default RHEL7 installation of Python - which is 2.7). Diagnostic Steps Run an innocuous job and observe the output from the job. If python version 2.x is specified as the version, it is clear that the venv is created without Python3. If not enough debug information is provided in your output, increment command line arguments from -v to up to -vvvv to get more granular feedback. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL

Last updated: 2024-08-05
Content: How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5.x Issue How is the python version in apache shipped modules updated? I would like to change the default Python that Apache is using. Resolution This can be accomplished on a per-script basis by adjusting the path to the python interpreter in the header. For instance: FROM: ```#!/usr/bin/python...``` TO: ```#!/usr/local/bin/python2.7...``` Adjusting this on a per-module basis, with mod_python or mod_wsgi is not supported. To adjust this you will need to recompile the module from source with the desired version of python. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Root Cause When doing a leapp upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 there is no warning or upgraded is prevented from proceeding forward when there are pacemaker resources configured that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 or no longer included in RHEL 10 controld , Filesystem resources for gfs2 filesystems. Content: How to tell if a PCI device is supported on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7, 8, or 9 Updated 4 Oct 2024 Notice RHEL6 provided a modules.pcimap catalog file that users could check to determine whether their devices were supported. This file is no longer available on RHEL 7 and later, but the modules.alias file can be a useful replacement. Caution! To determine if full support is available for a specific device, beyond just being recognized by the driver, check the set of release notes for the major version in question as to additional support qualifications. Under rare circumstances, some devices are included within drivers to allow unsupported recognition and use of specific devices for the convenience of our customers. For example, in RHEL 8.4 certain LSI storage controllers 1000:0071 was added back into the megaraid_sas driver, but per the prior Considerations in adopting RHEL 8, "11.1.2. Removed adapters" , these device is still unsupported. In addition, some devices recognized by a driver or some drivers themselves may only be in technical preview . Caution! As of 8.4 and later kernel versions, the modinfo alias pci output information for storage drivers is no longer sufficient by itself to determine specific device support status within the lpfc, qla*, megaraid_sas , mpt3sas , and aacraid drivers. ... Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. So the basic methodology to determine if a specific PCI card is recognized by a specific kernel version is a two step process: Method 1: Determine if driver has already been assigned Step 1: Use lspci -nnnvk and check card's output block for a driver: being present See " How to determine the make and model of HBA and version of associated driver from a RHEL Server? ... Under rare circumstances, some devices are included within drivers to allow unsupported recognition and use of specific devices for the convenience of our customers. For example, in RHEL 8.4 certain LSI storage controllers 1000:0071 was added back into the megaraid_sas driver, but per the prior Considerations in adopting RHEL 8, "11.1.2. Removed adapters" , these device is still unsupported. In addition, some devices recognized by a driver or some drivers themselves may only be in technical preview . Caution! As of 8.4 and later kernel versions, the modinfo alias pci output information for storage drivers is no longer sufficient by itself to determine specific device support status within the lpfc, qla*, megaraid_sas , mpt3sas , and aacraid drivers. ... Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. So the basic methodology to determine if a specific PCI card is recognized by a specific kernel version is a two step process: Method 1: Determine if driver has already been assigned Step 1: Use lspci -nnnvk and check card's output block for a driver: being present See " How to determine the make and model of HBA and version of associated driver from a RHEL Server? ... I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided search results discuss general RHEL package management and deprecations, but do not directly address how to install a package using `rpm-ostree`. `rpm-ostree` is designed for atomic updates and immutable systems, and direct package installation using `rpm-ostree install` (similar to `dnf install`) is generally not the primary method for adding software to the base system on these types of deployments.

In many cases, on systems that use `rpm-ostree` (like RHEL for Edge or Fedora CoreOS), you typically manage packages by creating custom container images or layering packages on top of the base image. For temporary installations that do not persist across reboots, RHEL 10 introduces `dnf --transient`.

Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 involves transferring its configuration and data. While there isn't a specific Red Hat guide solely for DHCP server migration between these versions, the general process involves backing up your RHEL 9 DHCP configuration, performing the upgrade to RHEL 10, and then restoring and verifying the configuration.

Here are the general steps:

1.  **Backup RHEL 9 DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Identify the DHCP server configuration files, typically `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Back up the DHCP lease database, usually located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.
    *   Back up any other relevant files, such as custom scripts or includes.
    *   Example commands for backing up:
        ```
        cp /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf /root/dhcpd.conf.backup
        cp /var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases /root/dhcpd.leases.backup
        ```

2.  **Perform RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 Upgrade:**
    *   Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `Leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. Review the documentation for specific considerations and prerequisites before starting the upgrade.
    *   Ensure your system meets the requirements and that all applications are compatible with RHEL 10. The upgrade process involves several steps, including pre-upgrade reports and potential remediations.

3.  **Install DHCP Server on RHEL 10 (if not automatically installed):**
    *   After the upgrade, verify if the DHCP server package (`dhcp-server`) is installed. If not, install it:
        ```
        dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

4.  **Restore DHCP Configuration and Leases:**
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.conf` to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.leases` to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.

5.  **Review and Adapt Configuration:**
    *   Check for any changes in the DHCP server package or configuration syntax between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10. Although major changes to the ISC DHCP server configuration are rare between minor RHEL releases, it's good practice to review the documentation for RHEL 10's `dhcpd` if available.
    *   Verify ownership and permissions of the restored files.

6.  **Start and Enable DHCP Service:**
    *   Start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```
        systemctl start dhcpd
        ```
    *   Enable the service to start on boot:
        ```
        systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

7.  **Verify Functionality:**
    *   Check the service status:
        ```
        systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Monitor `journalctl -u dhcpd` for any errors or warnings.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from a client to ensure it receives an IP address and other configuration details.

**Important Considerations from Red Hat Documentation:**

*   **`libdb` Removal:** The `libdb` package was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. While the ISC DHCP server typically uses its own lease file format, if your DHCP server relies on `libdb` for any custom functionality or database integration, you will need to migrate to a different key-value database before upgrading to RHEL 10.
*   **SHA-1 Deprecation:** SHA-1 has been deprecated in RHEL 9. If your DHCP configuration (though unlikely for DHCP itself) or any related components rely on SHA-1, you might encounter issues. Review the crypto policies and upgrade any affected components to use SHA-256 or stronger algorithms.

Since direct Red Hat documentation specifically detailing "migrate DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" was not found in the search results, the above steps are based on general best practices for migrating services during a major OS upgrade. query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 involves transferring its configuration and data. While there isn't a specific Red Hat guide solely for DHCP server migration between these versions, the general process involves backing up your RHEL 9 DHCP configuration, performing the upgrade to RHEL 10, and then restoring and verifying the configuration.

Here are the general steps:

1.  **Backup RHEL 9 DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Identify the DHCP server configuration files, typically `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Back up the DHCP lease database, usually located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.
    *   Back up any other relevant files, such as custom scripts or includes.
    *   Example commands for backing up:
        ```
        cp /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf /root/dhcpd.conf.backup
        cp /var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases /root/dhcpd.leases.backup
        ```

2.  **Perform RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 Upgrade:**
    *   Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `Leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. Review the documentation for specific considerations and prerequisites before starting the upgrade.
    *   Ensure your system meets the requirements and that all applications are compatible with RHEL 10. The upgrade process involves several steps, including pre-upgrade reports and potential remediations.

3.  **Install DHCP Server on RHEL 10 (if not automatically installed):**
    *   After the upgrade, verify if the DHCP server package (`dhcp-server`) is installed. If not, install it:
        ```
        dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

4.  **Restore DHCP Configuration and Leases:**
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.conf` to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.leases` to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.

5.  **Review and Adapt Configuration:**
    *   Check for any changes in the DHCP server package or configuration syntax between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10. Although major changes to the ISC DHCP server configuration are rare between minor RHEL releases, it's good practice to review the documentation for RHEL 10's `dhcpd` if available.
    *   Verify ownership and permissions of the restored files.

6.  **Start and Enable DHCP Service:**
    *   Start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```
        systemctl start dhcpd
        ```
    *   Enable the service to start on boot:
        ```
        systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

7.  **Verify Functionality:**
    *   Check the service status:
        ```
        systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Monitor `journalctl -u dhcpd` for any errors or warnings.

*   **`libdb` Removal:** The `libdb` package was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. While the ISC DHCP server typically uses its own lease file format, if your DHCP server relies on `libdb` for any custom functionality or database integration, you will need to migrate to a different key-value database before upgrading to RHEL 10.
*   **SHA-1 Deprecation:** SHA-1 has been deprecated in RHEL 9. If your DHCP configuration (though unlikely for DHCP itself) or any related components rely on SHA-1, you might encounter issues. Review the crypto policies and upgrade any affected components to use SHA-256 or stronger algorithms.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 involves transferring its configuration and data. While there isn't a specific Red Hat guide solely for DHCP server migration between these versions, the general process involves backing up your RHEL 9 DHCP configuration, performing the upgrade to RHEL 10, and then restoring and verifying the configuration.

Here are the general steps:

1.  **Backup RHEL 9 DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Identify the DHCP server configuration files, typically `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Back up the DHCP lease database, usually located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.
    *   Back up any other relevant files, such as custom scripts or includes.
    *   Example commands for backing up:
        ```
        cp /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf /root/dhcpd.conf.backup
        cp /var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases /root/dhcpd.leases.backup
        ```

2.  **Perform RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 Upgrade:**
    *   Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `Leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. Review the documentation for specific considerations and prerequisites before starting the upgrade.
    *   Ensure your system meets the requirements and that all applications are compatible with RHEL 10. The upgrade process involves several steps, including pre-upgrade reports and potential remediations.

3.  **Install DHCP Server on RHEL 10 (if not automatically installed):**
    *   After the upgrade, verify if the DHCP server package (`dhcp-server`) is installed. If not, install it:
        ```
        dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

4.  **Restore DHCP Configuration and Leases:**
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.conf` to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.leases` to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.

5.  **Review and Adapt Configuration:**
    *   Check for any changes in the DHCP server package or configuration syntax between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10. Although major changes to the ISC DHCP server configuration are rare between minor RHEL releases, it's good practice to review the documentation for RHEL 10's `dhcpd` if available.
    *   Verify ownership and permissions of the restored files.

6.  **Start and Enable DHCP Service:**
    *   Start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```
        systemctl start dhcpd
        ```
    *   Enable the service to start on boot:
        ```
        systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

7.  **Verify Functionality:**
    *   Check the service status:
        ```
        systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Monitor `journalctl -u dhcpd` for any errors or warnings.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from a client to ensure it receives an IP address and other configuration details.

**Important Considerations from Red Hat Documentation:**

*   **`libdb` Removal:** The `libdb` package was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. While the ISC DHCP server typically uses its own lease file format, if your DHCP server relies on `libdb` for any custom functionality or database integration, you will need to migrate to a different key-value database before upgrading to RHEL 10.
*   **SHA-1 Deprecation:** SHA-1 has been deprecated in RHEL 9. If your DHCP configuration (though unlikely for DHCP itself) or any related components rely on SHA-1, you might encounter issues. Review the crypto policies and upgrade any affected components to use SHA-256 or stronger algorithms.

Since direct Red Hat documentation specifically detailing "migrate DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" was not found in the search results, the above steps are based on general best practices for migrating services during a major OS upgrade. Always consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 documentation for the most accurate and up-to-date information regarding the `Leapp` upgrade process and any potential changes to the `dhcp-server` package.

    *   Example commands for backing up:
        ```
        cp /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf /root/dhcpd.conf.backup
        cp /var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases /root/dhcpd.leases.backup
        ```

2.  **Perform RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 Upgrade:**
    *   Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `Leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. Review the documentation for specific considerations and prerequisites before starting the upgrade.
    *   Ensure your system meets the requirements and that all applications are compatible with RHEL 10. The upgrade process involves several steps, including pre-upgrade reports and potential remediations.

3.  **Install DHCP Server on RHEL 10 (if not automatically installed):**
    *   After the upgrade, verify if the DHCP server package (`dhcp-server`) is installed. If not, install it:
        ```
        dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

4.  **Restore DHCP Configuration and Leases:**
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.conf` to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.leases` to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.

5.  **Review and Adapt Configuration:**
    *   Check for any changes in the DHCP server package or configuration syntax between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10. Although major changes to the ISC DHCP server configuration are rare between minor RHEL releases, it's good practice to review the documentation for RHEL 10's `dhcpd` if available.
    *   Verify ownership and permissions of the restored files.

6.  **Start and Enable DHCP Service:**
    *   Start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```
        systemctl start dhcpd
        ```
    *   Enable the service to start on boot:
        ```
        systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

7.  **Verify Functionality:**
    *   Check the service status:
        ```
        systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Monitor `journalctl -u dhcpd` for any errors or warnings.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from a client to ensure it receives an IP address and other configuration details.

**Important Considerations from Red Hat Documentation:**

*   **`libdb` Removal:** The `libdb` package was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. While the ISC DHCP server typically uses its own lease file format, if your DHCP server relies on `libdb` for any custom functionality or database integration, you will need to migrate to a different key-value database before upgrading to RHEL 10.
*   **SHA-1 Deprecation:** SHA-1 has been deprecated in RHEL 9. If your DHCP configuration (though unlikely for DHCP itself) or any related components rely on SHA-1, you might encounter issues. Review the crypto policies and upgrade any affected components to use SHA-256 or stronger algorithms.

Since direct Red Hat documentation specifically detailing "migrate DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" was not found in the search results, the above steps are based on general best practices for migrating services during a major OS upgrade. query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 involves transferring its configuration and data. While there isn't a specific Red Hat guide solely for DHCP server migration between these versions, the general process involves backing up your RHEL 9 DHCP configuration, performing the upgrade to RHEL 10, and then restoring and verifying the configuration.

Here are the general steps:

1.  **Backup RHEL 9 DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Identify the DHCP server configuration files, typically `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Back up the DHCP lease database, usually located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.
    *   Back up any other relevant files, such as custom scripts or includes.
    *   Example commands for backing up:
        ```
        cp /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf /root/dhcpd.conf.backup
        cp /var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases /root/dhcpd.leases.backup
        ```

2.  **Perform RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 Upgrade:**
    *   Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `Leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. Review the documentation for specific considerations and prerequisites before starting the upgrade.
    *   Ensure your system meets the requirements and that all applications are compatible with RHEL 10. The upgrade process involves several steps, including pre-upgrade reports and potential remediations.

3.  **Install DHCP Server on RHEL 10 (if not automatically installed):**
    *   After the upgrade, verify if the DHCP server package (`dhcp-server`) is installed. If not, install it:
        ```
        dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

4.  **Restore DHCP Configuration and Leases:**
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.conf` to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.leases` to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.

5.  **Review and Adapt Configuration:**
    *   Check for any changes in the DHCP server package or configuration syntax between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10. Although major changes to the ISC DHCP server configuration are rare between minor RHEL releases, it's good practice to review the documentation for RHEL 10's `dhcpd` if available.
    *   Verify ownership and permissions of the restored files.

6.  **Start and Enable DHCP Service:**
    *   Start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```
        systemctl start dhcpd
        ```
    *   Enable the service to start on boot:
        ```
        systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

7.  **Verify Functionality:**
    *   Check the service status:
        ```
        systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Monitor `journalctl -u dhcpd` for any errors or warnings.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from a client to ensure it receives an IP address and other configuration details.

**Important Considerations from Red Hat Documentation:**

*   **`libdb` Removal:** The `libdb` package was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. While the ISC DHCP server typically uses its own lease file format, if your DHCP server relies on `libdb` for any custom functionality or database integration, you will need to migrate to a different key-value database before upgrading to RHEL 10.
*   **SHA-1 Deprecation:** SHA-1 has been deprecated in RHEL 9. If your DHCP configuration (though unlikely for DHCP itself) or any related components rely on SHA-1, you might encounter issues. Review the crypto policies and upgrade any affected components to use SHA-256 or stronger algorithms.

Since direct Red Hat documentation specifically detailing "migrate DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" was not found in the search results, the above steps are based on general best practices for migrating services during a major OS upgrade. Always consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 documentation for the most accurate and up-to-date information regarding the `Leapp` upgrade process and any potential changes to the `dhcp-server` package.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 involves transferring its configuration and data. While there isn't a specific Red Hat guide solely for DHCP server migration between these versions, the general process involves backing up your RHEL 9 DHCP configuration, performing the upgrade to RHEL 10, and then restoring and verifying the configuration.

Here are the general steps:

1.  **Backup RHEL 9 DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Identify the DHCP server configuration files, typically `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Back up the DHCP lease database, usually located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.
    *   Back up any other relevant files, such as custom scripts or includes.
    *   Example commands for backing up:
        ```
        cp /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf /root/dhcpd.conf.backup
        cp /var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases /root/dhcpd.leases.backup
        ```

2.  **Perform RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 Upgrade:**
    *   Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `Leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. Review the documentation for specific considerations and prerequisites before starting the upgrade.
    *   Ensure your system meets the requirements and that all applications are compatible with RHEL 10. The upgrade process involves several steps, including pre-upgrade reports and potential remediations.

3.  **Install DHCP Server on RHEL 10 (if not automatically installed):**
    *   After the upgrade, verify if the DHCP server package (`dhcp-server`) is installed. If not, install it:
        ```
        dnf install dhcp-server
        ```

4.  **Restore DHCP Configuration and Leases:**
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.conf` to `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`.
    *   Copy the backed-up `dhcpd.leases` to `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`.

5.  **Review and Adapt Configuration:**
    *   Check for any changes in the DHCP server package or configuration syntax between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10. Although major changes to the ISC DHCP server configuration are rare between minor RHEL releases, it's good practice to review the documentation for RHEL 10's `dhcpd` if available.
    *   Verify ownership and permissions of the restored files.

6.  **Start and Enable DHCP Service:**
    *   Start the `dhcpd` service:
        ```
        systemctl start dhcpd
        ```
    *   Enable the service to start on boot:
        ```
        systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

7.  **Verify Functionality:**
    *   Check the service status:
        ```
        systemctl status dhcpd
        ```
    *   Monitor `journalctl -u dhcpd` for any errors or warnings.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from a client to ensure it receives an IP address and other configuration details.

**Important Considerations from Red Hat Documentation:**

*   **`libdb` Removal:** The `libdb` package was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. While the ISC DHCP server typically uses its own lease file format, if your DHCP server relies on `libdb` for any custom functionality or database integration, you will need to migrate to a different key-value database before upgrading to RHEL 10.
*   **SHA-1 Deprecation:** SHA-1 has been deprecated in RHEL 9. If your DHCP configuration (though unlikely for DHCP itself) or any related components rely on SHA-1, you might encounter issues. Review the crypto policies and upgrade any affected components to use SHA-256 or stronger algorithms.

Since direct Red Hat documentation specifically detailing "migrate DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" was not found in the search results, the above steps are based on general best practices for migrating services during a major OS upgrade. Always consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 documentation for the most accurate and up-to-date information regarding the `Leapp` upgrade process and any potential changes to the `dhcp-server` package.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Therefore, to set up a DHCP server in RHEL 10, you will need to install and configure Kea.

To install Kea DHCP server:

1.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server by editing its configuration files, typically located in `/etc/kea/`. The configuration involves defining subnets, IP address ranges, lease times, and other DHCP options.

For detailed configuration steps and examples for Kea DHCP server, please refer to the official Red Hat documentation for RHEL 10 regarding Kea.

Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Therefore, to set up a DHCP server in RHEL 10, you will need to install and configure Kea.

To install Kea DHCP server:

1.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server by editing its configuration files, typically located in `/etc/kea/`. The configuration involves defining subnets, IP address ranges, lease times, and other DHCP options.

For detailed configuration steps and examples for Kea DHCP server, please refer to the official Red Hat documentation for RHEL 10 regarding Kea.

Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Therefore, to set up a DHCP server in RHEL 10, you will need to install and configure Kea.

To install Kea DHCP server:

1.  **Install the Kea package:**
    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server by editing its configuration files, typically located in `/etc/kea/`. The configuration involves defining subnets, IP address ranges, lease times, and other DHCP options.

For detailed configuration steps and examples for Kea DHCP server, please refer to the official Red Hat documentation for RHEL 10 regarding Kea.

Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. With the introduction of Red Hat Enterprise Linux version 8, Red Hat simplified the RHEL product phases from four to three: Full Support, Maintenance Support, and Extended Life Phase. We are also providing information on anticipated release dates and minor releases that will offer extended support. Red Hat Enterprise Linux Version 8, 9, and 10 deliver a ten year life cycle in Full Support and Maintenance Support Phases followed by an Extended Life Phase. Life Cycle iv : The Red Hat Enterprise Linux life cycle phases are designed to reduce the level of change within each major release i over time and make release availability and content more predictable. ii Details During the life cycle for each major version, software changes to Red Hat Enterprise Linux are delivered via individual updates known as errata advisories through the Red Hat Customer Portal or other authorized Red Hat portals. ... No bug fixes, security fixes, hardware enablement or root-cause analysis will be available during this phase, and support will be provided on existing installations only. Red Hat reserves the right to terminate the ongoing support in the Extended Life Phase for a particular version of Red Hat Enterprise Linux at any time. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, 9, and 10 Life Cycle Red Hat is providing further predictability for Red Hat Enterprise Linux customers, partners, and ISVs. The below map provides details on when minor releases are planned, and which minor releases will include Extended Update Support and Update Services for SAP Solutions. Minor releases are planned for release every six months through the Full Support phase. RHEL 8 Planning Guide iv RHEL 9 Planning Guide iv RHEL 10 Planning Guide iv Red Hat Enterprise Linux Application Streams Life Cycle The vast majority of packages in Red Hat Enterprise Linux versions 8, 9, and 10, including most Application Streams, will be maintained for the full 10 year Red Hat Enterprise Linux life cycle. Specified life cycle components, however, will be maintained for less than 10 years, with a specified life cycle often matching the upstream life of the component. Application Streams will adhere to the errata criteria for the 10 year life cycle of the major release. For example, errata released for an Application Stream for RHEL 8, 9, or 10 during the Maintenance Support Phase will be confined to Critical, Important, and Moderate CVEs with a CVSS score of 7 or higher as defined by Red Hat in a Red Hat Security Advisory (RHSA) v ; and Urgent and Selected High Priority Bug Fix as defined by Red Hat in a Red Hat Bug Advisory (RHBA). Similarly, no new Application Streams will be released during the Maintenance Support Phase and Extended Life Phase since Software Enhancements are not permitted during these phases. However, to achieve the 10 years support criteria for RHEL, one version of each Application Stream will be identified at the start of the Maintenance Support Phase and that version will receive maintenance as described by the support phases. For example, most databases are supported upstream for 5 years and maintenance of their Application Stream in RHEL 8, 9, or 10 will align with their upstream support. There will be overlapping PostgreSQL Application Streams in RHEL 8, with each stream maintained for 5 years. ... **How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7128989

Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Customers running RHEL 9.0 through 9.3 will need to upgrade to at least RHEL 9.4 to obtain Python 3.12 for the control node. RHEL 8 can serve as both a control node and target node, but requires installing non-default Python versions from AppStream.
